data.alert_description: Verify and diagnose network reachability failure: source 192.168.0.12 cannot reach destination 172.16.0.12 (0/25 pings successful).

root_causes.cause: R2 has no route to the source network 192.168.0.0/24, so return traffic from 172.16.0.12 is not being sent back to R1/192.168.0.12.
evidence: R2 routing: only a 0.0.0.0/0 route present (fwd -> 10.0.0.6). (get_running_config for R2 -> routing.ip-route-interface-forwarding-list shows only prefix 0.0.0.0 fwd 10.0.0.6)
R1 LAN network: GigabitEthernet0/0/0 address 192.168.0.1/24 (source's subnet). (get_running_config for R1 -> interfaces.GigabitEthernet 0/0/0 address 192.168.0.1/255.255.255.0)
R1 has a route to 172.16.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.2 (so R1->R2 path exists), but R2 lacks a reciprocal route for 192.168.0.0/24.

root_causes.cause: Because R2 lacks a specific route to 192.168.0.0/24, its default route sends unknown-destination traffic to R3 (10.0.0.6). Replies from 172.16.0.12 are therefore routed toward R3 instead of back to R1, causing the ping failure.
evidence: R2 default route: 0.0.0.0/0 fwd 10.0.0.6. (get_running_config for R2 -> routing.ip-route-interface-forwarding-list entry fwd-list fwd = 10.0.0.6)
R3 Serial0/1/1 address is 10.0.0.6 (get_running_config for R3 -> Serial 0/1/1 primary address 10.0.0.6), so R2's default forwards toward R3 rather than R1.

R1's route to 172.16.0.0 uses next-hop 10.0.0.2 (R2). (get_running_config for R1 -> routing entry prefix 172.16.0.0 fwd-list fwd = 10.0.0.2) — traffic is forwarded toward R2 but replies follow R2's default to R3.

R3 Serial0/1/1 address is 10.0.0.6 (get_running_config for R3 -> Serial 0/1/1 primary address 10.0.0.6), so R2's default forwards toward R3 rather than R1.

R1's route to 172.16.0.0 uses next-hop 10.0.0.2 (R2). (get_running_config for R1 -> routing entry prefix 172.16.0.0 fwd-list fwd = 10.0.0.2) — traffic is forwarded toward R2 but replies follow R2's default to R3.

data.intent_description: R2 lacks a route to 192.168.0.0/24 so replies from 172.16.0.12 are forwarded via R2's default to R3 instead of back to R1; restore symmetric routing so source 192.168.0.12 can reach 172.16.0.12.

plan.problem: R2 has no route to 192.168.0.0/24; return traffic to 192.168.0.12 is sent to R3 via default route instead of R1.
plan.fix_summary: Add a static route on router2 for 192.168.0.0/24 pointing to R1 so return traffic is delivered to the source network.

plan.problem: R2 has no route to 192.168.0.0/24; return traffic to 192.168.0.12 is sent to R3 via default route instead of R1.
plan.fix_summary: Add a static route on router2 for 192.168.0.0/24 pointing to R1 so return traffic is delivered to the source network.

evidence: Most recent R2 running-config (tool: get_running_config) shows a specific route for 192.168.0.0/24 with next-hop 10.0.0.1 (routing.ip-route-interface-forwarding-list entry: prefix 192.168.0.0 mask 255.255.255.0 fwd-list fwd = 10.0.0.1).

R2 still has a default route (0.0.0.0/0 fwd 10.0.0.6) but the presence of the more specific 192.168.0.0/24 route means return traffic for that subnet will be forwarded to 10.0.0.1 (R1) instead of following the default — addressing the original asymmetric return-route issue.

R2 still has a default route (0.0.0.0/0 fwd 10.0.0.6) but the presence of the more specific 192.168.0.0/24 route means return traffic for that subnet will be forwarded to 10.0.0.1 (R1) instead of following the default — addressing the original asymmetric return-route issue.

messages.content:
What was observed
- Hosts on 192.168.0.0/24 could reach 172.16.0.0/24 via R1→R2, but replies from 172.16.0.12 were forwarded by R2 to R3 (default) instead of back to R1, causing asymmetric/launched failures.

Diagnosis (root cause)
- R2 had no route for 192.168.0.0/24, only a default 0.0.0.0/0 via R3, so return traffic to 192.168.0.12 followed the default to R3 instead of R1.

Changes made
- Added a static route on R2: 192.168.0.0/24 via 10.0.0.1 (R1).

Verification
- Passed. R2 running-config now contains a specific route 192.168.0.0/24 → 10.0.0.1; the 0.0.0.0/0 default remains but the more-specific route ensures replies to 192.168.0.0/24 go to R1.
- Remaining issues: none identified.

Verification

- Remaining issues: none identified.
